sentences.text: I Kina boede der engang en kejser, hvis palads var det smukkeste i hele verden.
sentences.translation: In China there once lived an emperor, whose palace was the most beautiful in the whole world.

words.grammar: hvis (possessive relative pronoun) · different word from hvis = "if" — context distinguishes them
et palads · paladset (the palace) · paladser (plural)

sentences.text: I skoven uden for paladset boede en lille, grå nattergal, som sang så smukt, at folk fra hele landet kom for at lytte.
sentences.translation: In the forest outside the palace lived a small, grey nightingale, which sang so beautifully that people from the whole country came to listen.

words.grammar: en nattergal · nattergalen (the nightingale) · nattergale (plural)
så + adjective/adverb...at + clause = result construction, expressing degree and consequence

sentences.text: Da kejseren endelig hørte fuglens sang, blev han dybt bevæget og bad om, at den skulle bo ved hoffet.
sentences.translation: When the emperor finally heard the bird's song, he was deeply moved and asked that it should live at the court.

words.grammar: en fugl · fuglen (the bird) · fuglens (the bird's, genitive) · -s added directly to the definite form
bevæget (common/neuter, same form) · past participle of bevæge, used as adjective · dybt bevæget = deeply moved

sentences.text: En dag fik kejseren en kunstig nattergal i gave, lavet af guld og dækket med ædelstene.
sentences.translation: One day the emperor received an artificial nightingale as a gift, made of gold and covered with precious stones.

words.grammar: kunstig (common) · kunstigt (neuter) · kunstige (pl./def.) · opposite of "ægte" (real/genuine)
en ædelsten · ædelstenen (the precious stone) · ædelstene (plural) · compound: ædel (noble) + sten (stone)

sentences.text: Den mekaniske fugl kunne kun synge én sang, men hoffet foretrak den, fordi den glimtede så flot.
sentences.translation: The mechanical bird could only sing one song, but the court preferred it, because it glittered so finely.

words.grammar: foretrække (to prefer) · foretrak (past) · har foretrukket (perfect) · strong/irregular verb, takes direct object
glimte (to glitter/sparkle) · glimtede (past) · har glimtet (perfect) · weak verb

sentences.text: Imens forsvandt den rigtige nattergal tilbage til skoven, ked af at være glemt.
sentences.translation: Meanwhile, the real nightingale disappeared back to the forest, sad about being forgotten.

words.grammar: imens (while/meanwhile) · can open a sentence as a time connector between two parallel events
forsvinde (to disappear) · forsvandt (past) · er forsvundet (perfect) · strong verb

sentences.text: Mange år senere blev kejseren alvorligt syg, og Døden satte sig på hans bryst.
sentences.translation: Many years later the emperor became seriously ill, and Death sat down on his chest.

words.grammar: alvorlig (serious, adj.) · alvorligt (adverb/neuter form)
et bryst · brystet (the chest) · bryster (plural)

sentences.text: Den kunstige fugl kunne ikke synge, for ingen havde trukket dens fjeder op.
sentences.translation: The artificial bird could not sing, because no one had wound up its spring.

words.grammar: trække op (to wind up) · trak op (past) · havde trukket op (pluperfect) · action completed before another past moment
dens (possessive for en-words/common gender, non-personal) · dets (for et-words)

sentences.text: Pludselig hørtes en sang udenfor vinduet: den rigtige nattergal var vendt tilbage.
sentences.translation: Suddenly a song was heard outside the window: the real nightingale had returned.

words.grammar: høre (to hear) · hørtes (passive -s form, past) · alternative to "blev hørt"
vende tilbage (to return) · vendte tilbage (past) · var vendt tilbage (pluperfect, takes "var" not "havde")

sentences.text: Den sang om kirkegårde, hvor man kan hvile, og om livet, som er værd at leve.
sentences.translation: It sang about churchyards, where one can rest, and about life, which is worth living.

words.grammar: hvor (relative adverb, place) · introduces a clause describing a location
værd (invariable) · være værd at + infinitive = to be worth doing

sentences.text: Døden blev bevæget af sangen og forsvandt langsomt ud i den kolde nat.
sentences.translation: Death was moved by the song and slowly disappeared out into the cold night.

words.grammar: kold (common) · koldt (neuter) · kolde (pl./def.)

sentences.text: Kejseren blev rask igen, og fra da af bad han altid nattergalen om at blive hos ham som ven, ikke som fange.
sentences.translation: The emperor became well again, and from then on he always asked the nightingale to stay with him as a friend, not as a prisoner.

words.grammar: fra da af (fixed time expression) · marks a lasting change starting at a specific past moment
en fange · fangen (the prisoner) · fanger (plural)

metadata.adaptedFrom: Adapted from H.C. Andersen (1843), simplified for B1 learners

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: ...en kejser, hvis palads var det smukkeste i hele verden.
grammarPoints.explanation: Hvis as a relative pronoun means "whose" and links a noun back to something it possesses. It looks identical to the conditional hvis ("if") but is always followed by a noun, not a full new clause.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: ...en nattergal, som sang så smukt, at folk fra hele landet kom for at lytte.
grammarPoints.explanation: Så + adjective/adverb sets up a degree, and at + clause delivers the consequence — together they express "so [X] that [Y happened]".

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Ingen havde trukket dens fjeder op. / Den rigtige nattergal var vendt tilbage.
grammarPoints.explanation: Har/havde or er/var (depending on the verb) + past participle marks an action completed before another point already in the past — one step further back than simple past.
This set's recurring blive pattern now combines with af to show passive cause: "became moved by" — emotional change of state triggered by something external.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Den sang om kirkegårde, hvor man kan hvile, og om livet, som er værd at leve.
grammarPoints.explanation: Two separate relative clauses, each attached to its own noun (kirkegårde / livet), can sit side by side in one sentence — common in more literary Danish.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Fra da af bad han altid nattergalen om at blive hos ham.
grammarPoints.explanation: This phrase means "from that point onward" and is used to mark a lasting change after a turning-point moment in a story.
